*Via Electronic Mail*

David Tanaka, Esq. Kesswick, Tanaka & Moreau LLP 51 West 52nd Street, 34th Floor New York, NY 10019

Thomas Buchanan III, Esq. Aldgate & Pryor LLP One Liberty Plaza, 42nd Floor New York, NY 10006

> Re: Intercreditor Agreement --- Positions of Ridgeline Capital Partners, LP, as Second Lien Agent --- Open Issues

Dear David and Thomas:

I write on behalf of our client, Ridgeline Capital Partners, LP (\"Ridgeline\"), in its capacity as Second Lien Agent under that certain Second Lien Credit Agreement, dated as of July 31, 2025 (the \"Second Lien Credit Agreement\"), among Meridian Industrial Holdings, Inc. (\"Meridian\" or the \"Borrower\"), Ridgeline, as administrative agent and collateral agent for the Second Lien Lenders (the \"2L Agent\"), and the participating second lien lenders party thereto from time to time (collectively, the \"Second Lien Lenders\"). As you are aware, I am a partner in the Finance & Restructuring Group at Sternfeld Yates & Carroll LLP, and this firm serves as counsel to Ridgeline in connection with the transactions described herein.

This letter addresses the ongoing negotiation of the Intercreditor Agreement, dated as of July 31, 2025 (the \"ICA\"), among Meridian, as Borrower, Cartwright National Bank, N.A. (\"Cartwright\"), as administrative agent and collateral agent for the First Lien Lenders (the \"1L Agent\"), and Ridgeline, as 2L Agent. Negotiations on the ICA commenced on May 12, 2025, and the ICA is intended to govern the intercreditor relationship between the First Lien Obligations---arising under the \$475,000,000 First Lien Credit Facility, which comprises a \$125,000,000 revolving credit facility (the \"Revolver\") and a \$350,000,000 Term Loan A (together, the \"First Lien Facility\"), each administered by Cartwright as 1L Agent---and the Second Lien Obligations, arising under the \$150,000,000 Second Lien Term Loan B (the \"Second Lien Facility\"). Together, the First Lien Facility and the Second Lien Facility constitute Meridian\'s secured capital structure, with Total Secured Debt of \$625,000,000.

The purpose of this letter is to set forth Ridgeline\'s positions on the four contested ICA provisions that remain unresolved and to articulate the rationale supporting each of those positions, with a view toward resolving the current impasse and meeting the July 31, 2025 drop-dead date for closing. As of the date of this letter, thirty-eight (38) days remain until that deadline. We note with urgency that Meridian\'s existing credit facility matures on August 15, 2025, leaving the Borrower no margin for delay. A failure to close the refinancing on or before the drop-dead date would place Meridian in an untenable position and could precipitate the very credit events that each party to this negotiation seeks to avoid.

Ridgeline\'s positions, as described in detail below, reflect its arm\'s-length analysis of current market precedent, the structural economics of the proposed capital structure, and alignment with the Borrower\'s legitimate operational and financial interests. In particular, we draw attention to the following economic realities of the proposed transaction: Total Secured Debt of \$625,000,000 is being layered against an appraised total enterprise value (\"TEV\") of approximately \$640,000,000, yielding a Total Loan-to-Value ratio (\"Total LTV\") of approximately 97.66%. The First Lien Obligations of \$475,000,000 represent a First Lien LTV of approximately 74.22%, leaving the Second Lien Lenders with an equity cushion (measured as the difference between TEV and First Lien Debt as a percentage of Second Lien Debt) of only approximately 25.78%---or approximately \$165,000,000 in absolute terms. These figures are not merely academic; they inform every position set forth in this letter, because the thinness of the Second Lien cushion means that the structural protections available to the Second Lien Lenders in the ICA are the primary safeguard of their investment.

Nothing in this letter constitutes a waiver of any rights, remedies, or defenses available to Ridgeline, the 2L Agent, or any Second Lien Lender under the Second Lien Credit Agreement, the ICA (in whatever form ultimately executed), or applicable law. All rights are expressly reserved.

We address each of the four open issues in turn below.

**[I. Standstill Period (Section 3.01(b) of the ICA)]{.underline}**

**A. Ridgeline\'s Position**

Ridgeline proposes that the standstill period set forth in Section 3.01(b) of the ICA be ninety (90) days following delivery of a Second Lien Enforcement Notice to the 1L Agent (the \"Standstill Period\"). During the Standstill Period, the 2L Agent and the Second Lien Lenders would be precluded from exercising remedies against the Collateral, subject to the customary exceptions for the filing of protective claims and the preservation of rights. At the expiration of the Standstill Period, the 2L Agent would be free to exercise any and all remedies available to the Second Lien Lenders under the Second Lien Credit Agreement, the security documents, and applicable law.

Critically, the Standstill Period must be measured from the date of actual receipt by the 1L Agent of the Second Lien Enforcement Notice, not from the date of occurrence of the underlying event of default giving rise to the notice. This distinction is important: the 2L Agent cannot be expected to bear the consequences of any delay in the identification, investigation, or confirmation of an event of default prior to the delivery of its enforcement notice. The trigger for the standstill must be the formal commencement of the enforcement process, which is the delivery of notice, not the potentially ambiguous and fact-dependent moment at which a default first arises.

**B. Market Support**

Ridgeline\'s position is firmly grounded in current market practice. Our internal market survey examined thirty-seven (37) sponsor-backed first lien / second lien intercreditor agreements in leveraged transactions that closed between January 2022 and December 2024. The survey focused on transactions in the industrial and manufacturing sectors with total funded secured debt in excess of \$400,000,000 and total leverage ratios exceeding 5.0x. The results demonstrate that a 90-day standstill period is squarely within the market range for transactions of comparable leverage and structure.

Standstill periods in the surveyed population ranged from 90 to 180 days, with a median of 120 days and a mode of 90 days. Notably, in transactions where total leverage exceeded 6.0x EBITDA---a category that includes Meridian, whose Total Leverage of approximately 6.99x places it at the higher end of the leverage spectrum---a significant cluster of agreements employed a 90-day standstill. Specifically, of the fourteen (14) surveyed transactions with total leverage in excess of 6.0x, nine (9) employed a 90-day standstill, three (3) employed a 120-day standstill, and two (2) employed a 150-day standstill. None of the surveyed transactions at that leverage level employed a standstill period of 270 days or anything approaching that duration.

The data relied upon in our survey are consistent with publicly available compilations from S&P LCD and Debtwire, both of which track intercreditor agreement terms in the leveraged loan market. We would be pleased to share our survey methodology and anonymized results with counsel on a professionals\'-eyes-only basis if that would assist in resolving this issue.

**C. Rationale --- Collateral Value Erosion**

The 270-day standstill period proposed by the 1L Agent is, in Ridgeline\'s view, fundamentally inconsistent with the economics of the Second Lien position and would expose the Second Lien Lenders to material and potentially irrecoverable collateral value erosion.

Meridian\'s collateral base consists principally of manufacturing equipment (including CNC machining centers, industrial presses, and automated assembly lines), specialized inventory (including work-in-process and finished goods with limited shelf life and customer-specific specifications), and owned real property (including the Borrower\'s primary manufacturing facility in Akron, Ohio, the distribution center in Greenville, South Carolina, and the corporate headquarters in Charlotte, North Carolina). Each of these asset categories is subject to significant depreciation, operational deterioration, and market risk during any period of financial distress.

Manufacturing equipment depreciates on an accelerated basis under distressed conditions, as deferred maintenance, reduced utilization, and the uncertainty surrounding the enterprise\'s viability all contribute to accelerated value decline. Specialized inventory---particularly work-in-process tied to specific customer contracts---can become obsolete or unsaleable if the Borrower\'s customer relationships deteriorate during a prolonged enforcement standstill. Owned real property, while generally more stable, is nonetheless subject to market risk and can decline in value if the Borrower\'s operational presence at those locations is diminished or eliminated.

With an equity cushion of only 25.78% (\$165,000,000 in absolute terms against a TEV of \$640,000,000), even modest deterioration in collateral value during a prolonged standstill could eliminate the Second Lien Lenders\' recovery entirely. To illustrate the magnitude of the risk: if Meridian\'s TEV were to decline by 25.78% over a 270-day standstill period---a scenario that is far from implausible for a highly leveraged industrial borrower in financial distress---the Second Lien Lenders\' recovery would be reduced to zero. A TEV decline of even 15% (to approximately \$544,000,000) would reduce the Second Lien Lenders\' theoretical recovery to approximately \$69,000,000, or 46 cents on the dollar, before accounting for the costs and frictions of a collateral liquidation.

A 90-day Standstill Period strikes the appropriate balance between the legitimate interests of the two lien levels. It affords the 1L Agent and Cartwright\'s syndicate meaningful time---a full calendar quarter---to pursue their own remedies, negotiate a consensual resolution with the Borrower, or evaluate a restructuring framework. At the same time, it prevents the standstill mechanism from being deployed tactically to indefinitely block Second Lien enforcement and effectively transform a lien subordination into a payment subordination. The distinction between these two concepts is fundamental to the intercreditor framework: the Second Lien Lenders have agreed to subordination of their liens, not subordination of their right to payment or their right to enforce their security interests at the appropriate time.

Ridgeline is deeply concerned that an extended standstill of 270 days---nearly nine months---when paired with the 1L Agent\'s proposed unilateral ability to extend its own enforcement actions (and thereby reset the standstill clock under certain formulations of the provision), would permit the First Lien Lenders to run out the clock on Second Lien remedies while collateral value declines. In practical effect, such a provision would convert the standstill from a temporary pause on 2L enforcement into a de facto preclusion of Second Lien rights. That result is not what the parties bargained for, is not supported by market precedent, and is not consistent with the economic terms on which the Second Lien Lenders agreed to provide \$150,000,000 of capital to this transaction.

**D. Borrower Alignment**

Ridgeline\'s position on the standstill is also aligned with the expressed preferences of Meridian\'s management. In discussions among the parties, Meridian\'s CEO, Sandra Koh-Wexler, and its CFO have indicated a preference for a capital structure that incentivizes prompt resolution of defaults rather than prolonged enforcement limbo. A 90-day standstill serves that objective by creating a defined and reasonable window for consensual resolution, after which the possibility of Second Lien enforcement action introduces constructive urgency into the workout process.

An extended standstill, by contrast, creates prolonged uncertainty for the Borrower\'s operations, its relationships with key customers and suppliers, and its ability to retain critical employees. In Meridian\'s industry---precision industrial components manufacturing---customer confidence in the continuity of supply is essential, and suppliers of specialized raw materials and components require assurance of payment continuity. A 270-day enforcement limbo would undermine those relationships, which in turn would accelerate the very collateral deterioration that harms both lien levels. The interests of the First Lien Lenders, the Second Lien Lenders, and the Borrower are aligned on this point: a shorter, defined standstill period promotes the stability and value preservation that benefit all stakeholders.

**[II. Second Lien Purchase Right (Section 5.01 of the ICA)]{.underline}**

**A. Purchase Price**

Ridgeline proposes that the purchase price payable upon exercise of the Second Lien purchase right (the \"Purchase Price\") be the lesser of:

> \(x\) **par**, defined as one hundred percent (100%) of the outstanding principal amount of the First Lien Obligations, plus accrued and unpaid interest thereon calculated at the non-default contract rate, plus any unreimbursed fees and expenses then due and owing to the 1L Agent under the First Lien Credit Agreement (but excluding any default interest, late charges, or other punitive amounts); and
>
> \(y\) **fair market value**, as determined by an independent dealer poll conducted by a nationally recognized dealer in syndicated loans selected by the 2L Agent (and reasonably acceptable to the 1L Agent), with a minimum of three (3) dealer quotations, such poll to be conducted within five (5) business days following delivery of the Second Lien Purchase Notice by the 2L Agent to the 1L Agent (such value, \"FMV\").

The accrued interest component included in the par calculation under clause (x) above should be calculated at the non-default contract rate applicable to the First Lien Obligations. Under the First Lien Credit Agreement, the applicable interest rate for the Term Loan A is SOFR plus 250 basis points. Based on the June 27, 2025 SOFR benchmark rate of 5.33%, the current all-in contract rate is approximately 7.83% per annum. Ridgeline\'s position is that default interest---which typically includes an additional 200 basis points above the contract rate---should not be included in the purchase price calculation, as the purpose of the purchase right is to allow the Second Lien Lenders to acquire the First Lien Obligations at their economic value, not to compensate the First Lien Lenders for punitive charges that may or may not be enforceable or appropriate under the circumstances.

By way of illustration, on a 90-day accrual basis applied to the full \$475,000,000 First Lien Facility (assuming full draw on both the Revolver and the Term Loan A), accrued interest at the non-default contract rate of 7.83% per annum would be approximately \$9,300,000 (calculated as \$475,000,000 × 0.0783 × 90/365 ≈ \$9,168,000, rounded for illustrative purposes to \$9,300,000). This would yield an illustrative par purchase price of approximately \$484,300,000, before the addition of any unreimbursed 1L Agent fees and expenses.

**B. Economic Impracticability of Par at Full Draw**

The 1L Agent\'s current proposal requires the Second Lien Lenders to pay par---and only par---as the purchase price for the First Lien Obligations, with no FMV alternative and with the inclusion of default-rate interest in the accrued interest calculation. Ridgeline submits that a par-only purchase price construct renders the purchase right economically impracticable in precisely the distressed scenarios in which the right is most needed and most likely to be exercised.

At full draw, the par purchase price (exclusive of default interest) would require the Second Lien Lenders to deploy approximately \$484,300,000 to acquire the First Lien Obligations---obligations that are senior to the Second Lien Lenders\' existing \$150,000,000 position. The total capital at risk for the Second Lien Lenders following exercise of the purchase right would therefore be approximately \$634,300,000, a figure that exceeds three times their original Second Lien exposure and approaches the total appraised enterprise value of the Borrower (\$640,000,000). In a distressed scenario---where the Borrower\'s TEV may have already declined below the appraised value---requiring the Second Lien Lenders to deploy capital in excess of the enterprise value to acquire the First Lien position would be economically irrational.

The Second Lien purchase right is a standard feature of intercreditor agreements, and its purpose is well understood: it provides the Second Lien Lenders with a protective mechanism to acquire the senior position in circumstances where the Second Lien Lenders believe they can maximize the value of their combined position through a consolidated approach to the credit---whether through a restructuring, a sale of the business, or a continuation of the enterprise. The purchase right is not designed to function as a windfall for the First Lien Lenders by guaranteeing them a par recovery on obligations that may be trading at a significant discount to face value due to the credit distress that triggered the enforcement scenario.

The FMV alternative, determined through an independent dealer poll conducted by a nationally recognized dealer with a minimum of three quotations, introduces an objective, market-based valuation mechanism that addresses the concerns of both sides. It protects the First Lien Lenders against below-value transfers by ensuring that the purchase price reflects the market\'s assessment of the First Lien Obligations\' value. At the same time, it ensures that the purchase right remains a viable and exercisable tool for the Second Lien Lenders, which is the fundamental purpose for which the right exists in the intercreditor framework.

**C. Unfunded Revolver Commitments**

Ridgeline\'s position on the purchase right also addresses a critical ancillary issue: the treatment of unfunded commitments under the \$125,000,000 First Lien Revolver. The 1L Agent\'s current draft of Section 5.01 would require the purchaser under the purchase right to assume all unfunded revolving credit commitments in addition to acquiring the funded and accrued First Lien Obligations. Ridgeline strongly objects to this requirement.

The Second Lien Lenders are term loan participants. Their investment thesis, credit analysis, and capital allocation were premised on a defined, fully funded exposure to the Borrower in the amount of \$150,000,000. Ridgeline\'s fund documents---including its Amended and Restated Agreement of Limited Partnership, dated as of March 1, 2021 (the \"Limited Partnership Agreement\")---contain structural constraints on the types of credit obligations that Ridgeline and its participating lenders may assume. Specifically, the Limited Partnership Agreement restricts the fund\'s ability to enter into open-ended commitment obligations that would require future capital calls on an uncertain and potentially accelerated schedule. Revolving credit commitments are precisely the type of open-ended obligation that these structural constraints are designed to address.

Mandatory assumption of unfunded revolver commitments would fundamentally transform the purchase right from a credit acquisition mechanism---in which the Second Lien Lenders acquire a defined and ascertainable set of obligations---into a commitment to provide new money to the Borrower on an uncertain future funding schedule, at the Borrower\'s discretion, in amounts that could total up to \$125,000,000 in additional funded exposure. That is a fundamentally different economic proposition from the acquisition of existing funded debt, and it is not a proposition that the Second Lien Lenders bargained for or that should be imposed through an intercreditor purchase right.

Accordingly, Ridgeline\'s position is that the purchase right should cover funded and accrued obligations only. The 1L Revolver lenders\' unfunded commitments are a function of their own credit decision, and those commitments should not be imposed on a purchaser under the intercreditor purchase right. Upon exercise of the purchase right, the unfunded revolver commitments would either terminate (as the revolving lenders would be made whole on their funded exposure) or would be addressed through a separate negotiation between the purchaser and the revolving lenders, outside the four corners of the ICA.

**[III. Permitted Second Lien Refinancing Indebtedness (Section 2.03(c) of the ICA)]{.underline}**

**A. Principal Amount**

Ridgeline proposes that Permitted Second Lien Refinancing Indebtedness---*i.e.*, indebtedness incurred by the Borrower to refinance all or a portion of the Second Lien Term Loan B---be permitted in an aggregate principal amount of up to one hundred twenty percent (120%) of the original principal amount of the Second Lien Term Loan B being refinanced. Applied to the current Second Lien Facility, this would permit refinancing indebtedness of up to \$180,000,000 (calculated as \$150,000,000 × 1.20 = \$180,000,000), representing a maximum incremental increase of \$30,000,000, or twenty percent (20%) of the original Second Lien principal.

The twenty percent (20%) headroom above the original principal amount is necessary to accommodate the customary costs and frictions associated with refinancing transactions in the leveraged loan market. These costs include, without limitation:

> \(i\) call premiums or make-whole amounts payable to the existing Second Lien Lenders upon prepayment of the Second Lien Term Loan B prior to its stated maturity (the Second Lien Credit Agreement provides for a call premium of 102% in year one, 101% in year two, and par thereafter);
>
> \(ii\) arranger fees and underwriting discounts payable to the arranging banks or placement agents for the replacement facility, which in the current market for second lien term loans typically range from 200 to 350 basis points of the committed amount;
>
> \(iii\) original issue discount (\"OID\") on the replacement facility, which, depending on market conditions at the time of refinancing, could range from 97 to 99 cents on the dollar for a facility of this type and leverage profile; and
>
> \(iv\) legal, advisory, and other transaction costs associated with the refinancing, including fees of counsel to the Borrower, counsel to the arranger, and counsel to the 2L Agent, as well as rating agency fees and filing costs.

Without the flexibility to incur refinancing indebtedness in an amount sufficient to cover these customary costs, the Second Lien Lenders would be economically locked into the existing Second Lien Facility regardless of changes in market conditions, the Borrower\'s credit profile, or the availability of more favorable refinancing terms. This result would defeat the fundamental purpose of a refinancing permission, which is to allow the parties to take advantage of improved market conditions or changed circumstances to reduce the Borrower\'s cost of capital and extend the maturity profile of its debt.

The 1L Agent\'s current proposal would cap Permitted Second Lien Refinancing Indebtedness at one hundred percent (100%) of the outstanding principal amount of the Second Lien Obligations at the time of refinancing. As a practical matter, this would prohibit any refinancing that involves non-trivial transaction costs, which is to say, virtually every refinancing transaction in the market.

**B. Maturity Date**

Ridgeline proposes that the maturity date of any Permitted Second Lien Refinancing Indebtedness be no earlier than June 15, 2031, which is the original stated maturity date of the Second Lien Term Loan B under the Second Lien Credit Agreement.

This proposal fully addresses the First Lien Lenders\' legitimate interest in ensuring that the Second Lien Obligations do not mature prior to the First Lien Obligations. The original Second Lien maturity date of June 15, 2031 already exceeds the First Lien maturity date of June 15, 2030 by a full twelve (12) months. Accordingly, the original maturity date also exceeds, by approximately nine (9) months, the \"91 days after the 1L maturity\" floor proposed by the 1L Agent (which would be September 14, 2030, calculated as 91 days after June 15, 2030).

There is no structural justification for requiring the refinanced Second Lien facility to mature later than the original Second Lien facility. The original maturity date already provides the First Lien Lenders with the full protection of their priority and temporal cushion. Requiring an even later maturity for refinancing indebtedness---as implied by the 1L Agent\'s proposal that the refinancing maturity be at least 91 days after the then-applicable 1L maturity---could have the perverse effect of discouraging refinancings that would otherwise benefit the Borrower and the capital structure as a whole, by requiring the Second Lien Lenders to accept an artificially extended maturity as the price of obtaining improved pricing or other favorable terms.

**C. Restrictiveness of Covenants**

Ridgeline\'s position is that no \"no more restrictive\" or \"not materially more restrictive\" test should apply to the covenants, representations, warranties, events of default, or other terms and conditions of any Permitted Second Lien Refinancing Indebtedness.

The 1L Agent\'s current draft of Section 2.03(c)(iii) includes a requirement that the terms of any Permitted Second Lien Refinancing Indebtedness be \"not materially more restrictive to the Borrower\" than the terms of the existing Second Lien Credit Agreement. Ridgeline objects to this provision for the following reasons.

First, the terms of the Second Lien facility---including financial maintenance covenants, affirmative covenants, negative covenants, events of default, and related provisions---are matters negotiated between the Second Lien Lenders and the Borrower and are governed exclusively by the Second Lien Credit Agreement. The ICA governs the intercreditor relationship between the two lien levels; it does not and should not regulate the bilateral commercial terms of the Second Lien facility itself.

Second, the ICA already provides the First Lien Lenders with the full suite of structural protections that the intercreditor framework is designed to deliver: lien subordination (ensuring that the First Lien Lenders\' security interests are senior in all respects to the Second Lien Lenders\' security interests), payment subordination during enforcement (ensuring that proceeds of collateral are applied first to the First Lien Obligations), the standstill mechanism (providing the First Lien Lenders with a defined period in which to exercise their senior remedies without interference), and the turnover and application-of-proceeds provisions (ensuring the correct waterfall of distributions). These protections are the purpose of the ICA. Additional restrictions on the commercial terms of the Second Lien facility are unnecessary and unwarranted.

Third, imposing a \"not materially more restrictive\" test on Second Lien refinancing indebtedness would give the 1L Agent an effective veto over the commercial terms of the Second Lien facility, because any proposed refinancing would require the 1L Agent\'s concurrence that the terms of the replacement facility satisfy the restrictiveness test. The standard is inherently subjective---reasonable parties may disagree as to whether a particular covenant formulation is \"materially more restrictive\"---and disputes over the application of the standard could delay or prevent a refinancing that would otherwise benefit the Borrower and the capital structure. This result is inconsistent with the intercreditor framework, which is premised on a separation of commercial terms (governed by the respective credit agreements) and intercreditor terms (governed by the ICA).

Fourth, the imposition of a restrictiveness test on second lien refinancing indebtedness is not supported by market precedent for transactions at this leverage level. In our review of the thirty-seven surveyed transactions referenced in Section I.B above, only four (4) included a \"not materially more restrictive\" test on second lien refinancing indebtedness, and each of those four transactions had total leverage below 5.0x and involved a materially thicker equity cushion than the 25.78% cushion present in this transaction. At higher leverage levels, where the Second Lien Lenders\' risk is greater and their need for flexibility is correspondingly more acute, the market does not support imposing a restrictiveness test that effectively gives the First Lien Lenders a veto over Second Lien commercial terms.

**[IV. Application of Asset Disposition and Casualty Proceeds (Section 4.02 of the ICA)]{.underline}**

**A. Ridgeline\'s Position**

Ridgeline proposes that Section 4.02 of the ICA provide for the following treatment of net cash proceeds from asset dispositions and casualty events:

Net cash proceeds arising from dispositions of Collateral and from casualty events affecting the Collateral should first be applied in accordance with the reinvestment and mandatory prepayment mechanics set forth in the First Lien Credit Agreement, and any residual proceeds remaining after full application to the First Lien Obligations (including after the expiration of any applicable reinvestment period) should flow to the 2L Agent for application to the Second Lien Obligations on a pro rata basis among the Second Lien Lenders.

Specifically, the First Lien Credit Agreement provides for a three hundred sixty-five (365) day reinvestment period (the \"Reinvestment Period\") during which the Borrower may reinvest net cash proceeds in replacement assets used or useful in the Borrower\'s business, or may otherwise apply such proceeds in a manner consistent with the Borrower\'s capital expenditure program, in each case subject to the terms and conditions of the First Lien Credit Agreement. Upon expiration of the Reinvestment Period, the First Lien Credit Agreement requires a mandatory prepayment sweep of one hundred percent (100%) of any net cash proceeds not so reinvested, applied to the outstanding First Lien Obligations in accordance with the waterfall set forth therein. The First Lien Credit Agreement also provides for de minimis thresholds for asset sale mandatory prepayments: individual dispositions generating net cash proceeds of less than \$5,000,000 are excluded from the mandatory prepayment requirement, and the aggregate annual exclusion threshold is \$15,000,000.

Under Ridgeline\'s proposal, the ICA waterfall for asset disposition and casualty proceeds would operate as follows:

> \(i\) Net cash proceeds from asset dispositions and casualty events would first be applied in accordance with the reinvestment and mandatory prepayment mechanics set forth in the First Lien Credit Agreement, including the Borrower\'s right to reinvest during the Reinvestment Period and the mandatory prepayment sweep upon expiration of that period;
>
> \(ii\) After full satisfaction of all First Lien Obligations entitled to receive such proceeds under the First Lien Credit Agreement (or after the 1L Agent delivers written confirmation to the 2L Agent that no further application to the First Lien Obligations is required or that the First Lien Obligations have been paid in full), any residual proceeds remaining in the applicable collateral proceeds account would be released to the 2L Agent for application to the Second Lien Obligations in accordance with the terms of the Second Lien Credit Agreement.

**B. Rationale.** Ridgeline\'s proposal respects the priority of the First Lien Obligations while ensuring that proceeds that have fully satisfied the First Lien waterfall are not unnecessarily trapped at the Borrower level to the detriment of the next-priority secured creditors.

The First Lien Credit Agreement already contains negotiated thresholds and mechanics for the treatment of asset disposition and casualty proceeds that reflect the Borrower\'s operational needs and the First Lien Lenders\' credit judgment. The \$5,000,000 individual threshold and \$15,000,000 annual aggregate threshold were agreed between the Borrower and the First Lien Lenders in the context of the First Lien Credit Agreement negotiation, and they reflect a considered balance between the First Lien Lenders\' desire to capture asset sale proceeds for debt reduction and the Borrower\'s need for operational flexibility to manage its asset base in the ordinary course. The 365-day Reinvestment Period similarly reflects a negotiated balance, allowing the Borrower time to identify and acquire suitable replacement assets while ensuring that proceeds not reinvested within a reasonable period are applied to reduce First Lien Debt.

Ridgeline\'s proposal does not seek to override, modify, or in any way diminish these negotiated thresholds or reinvestment mechanics. The First Lien Lenders would receive the full benefit of their bargain under the First Lien Credit Agreement. Ridgeline\'s proposal simply provides that, once the First Lien waterfall has been fully satisfied---*i.e.*, once proceeds have been applied to the First Lien Obligations in the amounts and on the timeline specified in the First Lien Credit Agreement, and any residual remains---such residual proceeds are not trapped at the Borrower level but are instead applied to reduce the next-priority secured indebtedness.

The 1L Agent\'s counterproposal---a blanket one hundred percent (100%) sweep of all net cash proceeds from asset dispositions and casualty events to the First Lien Obligations, without regard to the reinvestment mechanics or mandatory prepayment thresholds set forth in the First Lien Credit Agreement---is objectionable for two independent reasons.

First, it would deprive the Borrower of operational flexibility that the First Lien Credit Agreement itself expressly grants. The reinvestment rights and de minimis thresholds in the First Lien Credit Agreement were negotiated to allow Meridian to maintain and modernize its industrial asset base---an essential requirement for a manufacturing business operating in competitive markets with demanding quality and throughput requirements. A blanket ICA sweep that overrides those provisions would effectively amend the First Lien Credit Agreement through the back door of the intercreditor agreement, a result that is both commercially unreasonable and legally problematic.

Second, a blanket sweep to the First Lien Obligations of proceeds that exceed the First Lien Credit Agreement\'s own mandatory prepayment requirements would trap residual value above the First Lien Obligations without any corresponding benefit to the First Lien Lenders. Once the First Lien Obligations have received the full benefit of the asset sale and casualty proceeds waterfall under their own credit agreement, additional proceeds swept to the Borrower\'s balance sheet (rather than applied to the Second Lien Obligations) would simply remain as unrestricted cash at the Borrower level---available for general corporate purposes but not dedicated to any particular creditor\'s benefit. Ridgeline\'s proposal achieves a more efficient outcome by directing residual proceeds to the reduction of the next-priority secured indebtedness, thereby deleveraging the capital structure for the benefit of all stakeholders.

Meridian\'s management has expressed support for an approach that respects the reinvestment mechanics agreed in the First Lien Credit Agreement. As Ms. Koh-Wexler has communicated to all parties, those mechanics were specifically negotiated to allow the Borrower to replace aging manufacturing equipment, upgrade production lines, and respond to shifts in customer demand without triggering mandatory prepayments that would deplete the Borrower\'s liquidity. A blanket ICA sweep provision would undermine those mechanics and, in the Borrower\'s view, would be inconsistent with the operational framework that the First Lien Lenders themselves agreed to when the First Lien Credit Agreement was negotiated.

**[V. Conclusion and Request for Response]{.underline}**

To summarize, Ridgeline\'s positions on the four open ICA provisions are as follows:

> \(1\) **Standstill Period (Section 3.01(b)):** Ninety (90) days from delivery of the Second Lien Enforcement Notice, measured from actual receipt by the 1L Agent.
>
> \(2\) **Purchase Price for Second Lien Purchase Right (Section 5.01):** The lesser of par (at the non-default contract rate) and fair market value determined by independent dealer poll, with no obligation to assume unfunded revolver commitments.
>
> \(3\) **Permitted Second Lien Refinancing Indebtedness (Section 2.03(c)):** Up to 120% of the original principal amount of the Second Lien Obligations being refinanced, with a maturity no earlier than the original Second Lien maturity (June 15, 2031), and with no \"not materially more restrictive\" covenant test.
>
> \(4\) **Application of Asset Disposition and Casualty Proceeds (Section 4.02):** Application in accordance with the First Lien Credit Agreement\'s reinvestment and mandatory prepayment mechanics, with residual proceeds flowing to the Second Lien Obligations.

These positions, taken together, represent a commercially reasonable framework that fully respects the priority of the First Lien Obligations and the structural protections to which the First Lien Lenders are entitled under the intercreditor framework, while preserving the Second Lien Lenders\' bargained-for economic protections---protections that are essential given the thin equity cushion and high leverage of this transaction. Ridgeline has not sought to diminish the First Lien Lenders\' priority, to interfere with the First Lien Credit Agreement\'s mechanics, or to obtain any protections that are inconsistent with market practice for transactions of comparable size, leverage, and structure.

Ridgeline is fully willing to engage in further negotiation on each of these provisions and to discuss the interplay among the four provisions as a package. We recognize that intercreditor negotiations involve trade-offs and that the resolution of any individual issue may be informed by the resolution of the others. We are prepared to approach these discussions constructively and with the flexibility that a package negotiation requires, provided that discussions proceed promptly in light of the July 31, 2025 drop-dead date.

We respectfully request that Cartwright and its counsel at Aldgate & Pryor LLP, and Meridian and its counsel at Kesswick, Tanaka & Moreau LLP, respond to this letter with their respective reactions and any counterproposals no later than **Monday, June 30, 2025**. That date would leave thirty-one (31) days before the drop-dead date---a period that, while tight, should be sufficient for the drafting of agreed language, internal credit committee approvals at Cartwright and within the Ridgeline fund structure, and final execution of the ICA and the related transaction documents.

We note that any further delay in resolving these open issues places the entire transaction timeline at risk. The consequences of failing to close by July 31, 2025---with the existing facility maturing on August 15, 2025---would be severe for all parties, but most immediately for the Borrower and for the First Lien Lenders under the existing facility who face the prospect of an unrefinanced maturity.

Ridgeline expressly reserves the right to revise, supplement, or withdraw any of the positions set forth in this letter as negotiations progress and as additional information becomes available. Nothing herein constitutes a binding commitment or an agreement to any particular term, and all positions are subject to the negotiation and execution of a mutually acceptable ICA in definitive form.

We look forward to hearing from you and to bringing these negotiations to a successful conclusion.

Very truly yours,

STERNFELD YATES & CARROLL LLP

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

Naomi Oduya Partner

Direct Dial: (212) 584-7148

Email: noduya@sternfeldyates.com

**cc:** (via electronic mail)

> Sandra Koh-Wexler, Chief Executive Officer, Meridian Industrial Holdings, Inc.
>
> Ridgeline Capital Partners, LP (Attention: Marcus J. Ridley, Managing Director and Head of Credit Investments)

**CONFIDENTIALITY NOTICE:** This communication, including any attachments, is from the law firm of Sternfeld Yates & Carroll LLP and is intended solely for the use of the individual(s) or entity(ies) to whom it is addressed. This communication may contain information that is privileged, confidential, and exempt from disclosure under applicable law, including attorney-client privileged communications and attorney work product. If you are not the intended recipient, or the employee or agent responsible for delivering this communication to the intended recipient, you are hereby notified that any dissemination, distribution, copying, or other use of this communication or its contents is strictly prohibited. If you have received this communication in error, please notify the sender immediately by telephone at (212) 584-7000 and permanently delete all copies of this communication and any attachments.

**Privileged & Confidential --- For Settlement Discussion Purposes Only.** This letter is submitted pursuant to Federal Rule of Evidence 408 and any applicable state-law equivalents. The positions, proposals, and analyses set forth herein are made solely for the purpose of facilitating settlement discussions among the parties with respect to the Intercreditor Agreement described herein. Nothing in this letter constitutes an admission of fact, an admission of liability, or a concession on any issue of law or fact, and this letter and its contents may not be offered or admitted into evidence in any judicial, arbitral, or administrative proceeding for any purpose other than to enforce the terms of any agreement reached as a result of the discussions contemplated herein.

From: Thomas Buchanan III <tbuchanan@aldgatepryor.com>
To: David Tanaka <dtanaka@ktmlaw.com>
Date: Thu, 26 Jun 2025 06:47:00 -0000
Subject: ICA Negotiations — Without Prejudice — Syndicate Position Update
Content-Type: text/plain; charset="utf-8"
Content-Transfer-Encoding: quoted-printable
MIME-Version: 1.0

PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL =E2=80=94 FOR SETTLEMENT DISCUSSION PURPOSES ONLY
WITHOUT PREJUDICE

David,

Thank you for the candid conversation on Tuesday. I appreciated the directnes=
s, and I think it confirmed what we both already knew =E2=80=94 we are at an =
impasse on several key ICA provisions and we need to find a way through it qu=
ickly.

Since our call, I have been back to Cartwright's credit committee to obtain c=
learer authorization on where the syndicate has genuine flexibility and where=
 it does not. I want to share that guidance with you informally and without p=
rejudice, in the hope that it helps us narrow the open issues before we sit d=
own again with the full group. I am deliberately not copying Naomi Oduya or a=
nyone at Sternfeld on this =E2=80=94 I will leave it to your judgment how and=
 what to share with the 2L side.

I do not need to belabor the calendar, but let me state it plainly for the re=
cord: the drop-dead date is July 31, 2025. That gives us approximately 34 day=
s. The existing facility matures August 15, 2025, and there is no realistic p=
rospect of an extension. We cannot afford slippage. We have been negotiating =
this ICA since May 12, and the time for positional bargaining has passed. Wha=
t follows is my best effort to give you a practical map of the terrain.

**Standstill Period (Section 3.01(b))**

The credit committee has approved a minimum standstill of 150 days. That is t=
he floor, David =E2=80=94 the committee will not authorize anything below it.=
 I recognize that our opening position was 270 days and Ridgeline's was 90 da=
ys, and I am aware of the market data suggesting a median of approximately 18=
0 days across comparable 2022=E2=80=932024 transactions. The syndicate could =
live with something in the 150- to 180-day range, but 150 is the hard floor.

On the related question of early termination events during the standstill: th=
e committee's strong preference remains that there be no early termination tr=
iggers during the standstill period. That said, if a longer standstill =E2=80=
=94 closer to 180 days =E2=80=94 were paired with carefully defined early ter=
mination triggers, that is something the committee would be willing to consid=
er. I will be candid that it is a harder sell internally, and the triggers wo=
uld need to be narrow and objectively verifiable, but the door is not closed.

**Second Lien Purchase Right =E2=80=94 Purchase Price (Section 5.01)**

I need to be direct here: **par purchase price for funded debt is non-negotia=
ble.** The syndicate will not accept any formulation that permits purchase be=
low par. The purchase price must include 100% of outstanding principal on all=
 funded 1L obligations =E2=80=94 the $475 million facility comprising the $12=
5 million Revolver and the $350 million Term Loan A =E2=80=94 plus accrued in=
terest, plus fees and expenses. This is a firm line from the credit committee=
 and there is no room to move.

However, there may be room on two subsidiary points:

*Default-rate interest.* Our opening position was that accrued interest in th=
e purchase price must be calculated at the default rate. I have some indicati=
on from the committee that there may be room to accept accrued interest calcu=
lated at the non-default contract rate =E2=80=94 SOFR plus 250 basis points, =
which currently yields an all-in rate of approximately 7.83% based on SOFR at=
 5.33%. To put illustrative numbers on it: on a 90-day accrual on $475 millio=
n at the non-default rate, the interest component would be approximately $9.3=
0 million, producing an illustrative par purchase price of approximately $484=
.30 million. The committee could potentially accept this formulation.

*Unfunded Revolver commitments.* Our opening position was mandatory assumptio=
n of unfunded Revolver commitments up to the $125 million commitment amount. =
I understand that Ridgeline has indicated its fund LPA documents create const=
raints around open-ended revolving credit commitment obligations =E2=80=94 I =
acknowledge that is a practical consideration, not a positional one. There ma=
y be room to soften this requirement, potentially making assumption of unfund=
ed Revolver commitments optional rather than mandatory. I cannot commit to th=
at yet, but I can tell you the committee is willing to discuss it.

**Permitted Second Lien Refinancing =E2=80=94 Restrictiveness Test (Section 2=
.03(c))**

Our opening position was a blanket "no more restrictive in any material respe=
ct" test applied to any 2L refinancing indebtedness. Ridgeline has pushed for=
 no restrictiveness test at all. Neither position is going to carry the day.

Cartwright is open to a **narrowed restrictiveness test** =E2=80=94 one that =
would not require a blanket comparison but would instead focus on specificall=
y enumerated provisions =E2=80=94 **provided it is combined with a most-favor=
ed-nation right** for the benefit of the 1L lenders. The MFN right would ensu=
re that if any 2L refinancing debt contains financial maintenance covenants m=
ore favorable than those in the 1L Credit Agreement, the 1L lenders receive t=
he benefit of those covenants automatically. For context, the existing 2L fin=
ancial maintenance covenant is Total Leverage not to exceed 6.5x. The credit =
committee views this pairing =E2=80=94 narrowed restrictiveness test plus MFN=
 =E2=80=94 as a reasonable middle ground, and I think there is a credible arg=
ument that it serves both sides' interests.

**Permitted Second Lien Refinancing =E2=80=94 Principal Amount Increase (Sect=
ion 2.03(c))**

For completeness: the 1L position remains that any 2L refinancing should not =
increase principal above the original $150 million 2L amount. I am aware that=
 Ridgeline has proposed up to a 20% increase, or $30 million. I am not in a p=
osition to signal specific flexibility on this point today, but I note our wi=
llingness to discuss it further in the context of the overall package. This w=
ill inevitably be part of the broader trade.

**Asset Sale Proceeds Waterfall (Section 4.02)**

A **hard sweep of proceeds from large and/or extraordinary asset sales is ess=
ential** to the syndicate and is not subject to significant compromise. That =
is a firm requirement.

That said, I acknowledge that reinvestment rights for smaller, ordinary-cours=
e asset sales may be acceptable. The syndicate recognizes that the Borrower's=
 business requires routine asset turnover, and it would be counterproductive =
to strip out all reinvestment flexibility. As you know, the 1L Credit Agreeme=
nt itself already contains reinvestment mechanics =E2=80=94 a 365-day reinves=
tment period, with individual asset sale prepayment triggers at $5 million an=
d annual aggregate triggers at $15 million, and a 100% sweep of non-reinveste=
d proceeds.

A workable approach might be for the ICA to defer to the 1L Credit Agreement'=
s existing reinvestment mechanics for ordinary-course sales below a defined t=
hreshold, while imposing a hard 100% sweep to 1L obligations for larger or ex=
traordinary dispositions. I would flag one important concern: any threshold d=
istinguishing ordinary-course from extraordinary sales must be carefully draf=
ted to prevent evasion =E2=80=94 specifically, to guard against a large dispo=
sition being structured as a series of smaller transactions. Anti-evasion lan=
guage will be essential.

As for the question of residual proceeds flowing to 2L after full 1L satisfac=
tion, that is something the syndicate would need to evaluate in context, but =
candidly it is less of a concern if the hard sweep for significant sales is p=
reserved.

**Next Steps**

I want to reiterate that everything above is informal guidance, communicated =
without prejudice, and subject to final syndicate and credit committee approv=
al of any complete package. Nothing here constitutes a binding commitment.

My hope is that this gives you enough to work with in formulating a comprehen=
sive compromise proposal. I would suggest we schedule a call early next week =
=E2=80=94 Monday or Tuesday =E2=80=94 to discuss your reaction and any counte=
rproposal before we reconvene with Naomi and the Ridgeline team. As you know,=
 all transaction documents are governed by New York law, and I think we are w=
ell-served by keeping this process moving expeditiously and in good faith.

I am available over the weekend if anything urgent arises. Do not hesitate to=
 call my cell.

Best regards,

Thomas Buchanan III
Partner
Aldgate & Pryor LLP
tbuchanan@aldgatepryor.com

---

ential, and intended solely for the use of the individual or entity to whom i=
t is addressed. This communication is made without prejudice and is intended =
for settlement discussion purposes only pursuant to Federal Rule of Evidence =
408 and its state-law equivalents. If you are not the intended recipient, ple=
ase notify the sender immediately and delete this message. Any unauthorized r=
eview, use, disclosure, or distribution is prohibited. Aldgate & Pryor LLP.

 comprehensive package resolution. To be clear, this is not a formal counter-=

ntercreditor Agreement (dated as of July 31, 2025; New York governing law) th=

**1. Standstill Period (Section 3.01(b))**

enced the KTM market survey (2022=E2=80=932024 data), which showed a median s=

Ridgeline can accept up to a **180-day standstill**, but only if the ICA incl=

hout 2L consent.

I understand that Aldgate & Pryor's position on behalf of the 1L Agent has be=
en no early termination events whatsoever. I want to flag this as the critica=
l gap on this issue. A bare 180-day standstill without triggers is not someth=
ing my client can accept =E2=80=94 the two components are linked.

**2. Purchase Right =E2=80=94 Purchase Price (Section 5.01)**

ening was the lesser of par or FMV via independent dealer poll.

ptable** as the Purchase Price for the 1L Obligations. This is a significant =

However, this acceptance is **conditioned on the unfunded revolver assumption=

 concern; Ridgeline simply cannot commit to assume unfunded portions of that =

Ridgeline would be willing to purchase all funded amounts at par. To put illu=
strative numbers on this: $475M of total 1L funded debt, plus accrued interes=
t at the non-default contract rate (SOFR + 250 bps; at current SOFR of 5.33%,=
 the all-in rate is 7.83%). On an illustrative 90-day accrual basis, that wou=
ld be approximately $9.30M in accrued interest, yielding an illustrative par =
purchase price of approximately $484.30M for funded debt.

We are also willing to discuss whether accrued interest at the non-default co=
ntract rate (rather than the default rate the 1L side insists upon) could be =
part of the overall package. But I want to be unambiguous: mandatory unfunded=
 revolver assumption is a deal-breaker given the LPA constraints I've describ=
ed.

**3. Permitted Second Lien Refinancing =E2=80=94 Principal Increase Cap (Sect=
ion 2.03(c))**

The 1L position has been a 0% cap (equal or lesser principal only); Ridgeline=
 opened at 20% ($30M on the $150M original 2L principal).

Ridgeline **can accept a 10% principal increase cap** =E2=80=94 that is, $15M=
 (calculated as $150M =C3=97 10%) =E2=80=94 provided the increase is **limite=
d to refinancing-related fees and costs** (e.g., OID, arrangement fees, and t=
ransaction expenses). This is a meaningful move from our opening position.

On maturity, we don't see this as contentious: the original 2L maturity of Ju=
ne 15, 2031 already exceeds the 91-day post-1L maturity floor (i.e., Septembe=
r 14, 2030, based on the 1L maturity of June 15, 2030).

We're also open to discussing whether a narrowed restrictiveness test paired =
with an MFN right could work =E2=80=94 I understand Sandra's team may have in=
dicated that Cartwright would accept something along those lines. The existin=
g 2L financial maintenance covenant is Total Leverage =E2=89=A4 6.5x, and any=
 MFN discussion should be contextualized against that benchmark.

**4. Asset Disposition Proceeds =E2=80=94 Waterfall and Residual (Section 4.0=
2)**

The 1L position has been a 100% mandatory sweep to the 1L overriding reinvest=
ment rights, with no 2L access. Ridgeline's position has been that the 1L Cre=
dit Agreement reinvestment mechanics should be followed, with residual procee=
ds flowing to the 2L. For reference, those mechanics include a $5M individual=
 / $15M annual aggregate asset sale prepayment trigger, a 365-day reinvestmen=
t period, and a 100% sweep of non-reinvested proceeds.

Ridgeline understands that hard sweeps on large or extraordinary asset sales =
may be necessary to protect the 1L group's position. Our primary concern is t=
hat **some form of 2L access to residual asset sale proceeds** be preserved i=
n the ICA. I'd characterize this as **symbolically important even if economic=
ally limited** =E2=80=94 the 2L lenders need to demonstrate to their own inve=
stors and LPs that the intercreditor structure preserves at least a theoretic=
al waterfall right after full 1L satisfaction.

Ridgeline could live with the 1L reinvestment mechanics applying for ordinary=
-course sales (I understand Cartwright may accept reinvestment rights for sma=
ller ordinary-course dispositions), provided that the residual after 1L appli=
cation flows to the 2L rather than back to the Borrower. I do want to flag on=
e concern: we would need anti-evasion language to protect against asset-strip=
ping through a series of sub-threshold sales structured to stay below the $20=
M ordinary-course threshold. We should discuss appropriate drafting on that f=
ront.

**5. Capital Structure Context**

For reference, and because it informs Ridgeline's perspective on all of the a=
bove: the total 1L Facility is $475M ($125M Revolver + $350M TLA); the 2L Ter=
m Loan B is $150M; total Secured Debt is $625M. The TEV per lender appraisal =
is approximately $640M, putting 1L LTV at 74.22% and Total LTV at 97.66% =E2=
=80=94 leaving the 2L with an equity cushion of just 25.78%. 1L Leverage is 5=
.31x and Total Leverage is 6.99x against LTM EBITDA of $89.4M. The 1L carries=
 SOFR + 250 bps (floating) with a June 15, 2030 maturity; the 2L carries SOFR=
 + 625 bps (floating) with a June 15, 2031 maturity.

That thin equity cushion is precisely why the 2L purchase right, standstill t=
erms, and proceeds waterfall are all the more consequential to Ridgeline's po=
sition. I raise these numbers not to belabor the obvious but to underscore wh=
y the flexibility signals above represent genuine movement by a client that i=
s keenly aware of the risk profile of its position.

---

David, to reiterate =E2=80=94 these are informal flexibility signals, not a f=
ormal counter-draft. Ridgeline's strong preference is that these points be ad=
dressed as a **single, integrated package** rather than piecemeal. I understa=
nd that Sandra Koh-Wexler has expressed willingness to signal Meridian's supp=
ort for compromise to both agents, and I think that would be genuinely helpfu=
l in moving things along.

I'm available to discuss any of this by phone or in person at your convenienc=
e. If you think it would be productive, I'd also be willing to sit down joint=
ly with you and Thomas Buchanan at Aldgate & Pryor to work through the packag=
e =E2=80=94 but I leave the sequencing to your judgment as Borrower's counsel.

Finally, I'd ask that you treat this communication as confidential and withou=
t prejudice, and that you not share it with counterparty counsel or the 1L Ag=
ent without my prior consent.

Thank you, David. I look forward to hearing your thoughts.

Best regards,

**Naomi Oduya**
Partner
Sternfeld Yates & Carroll LLP
900 Third Avenue, 25th Floor
New York, NY 10022
T: (212) 554-8120
M: (917) 483-2267
noduya@sternfeldyates.com

*This email may contain information that is privileged, confidential, or othe=
rwise protected from disclosure. If you are not the intended recipient, pleas=
e notify the sender immediately and delete this message.*

result:
From: Sandra Koh-Wexler <skohwexler@meridianindustrial.com>
To: David Tanaka <dtanaka@ktmlaw.com>
Cc: Rachel Fong <rfong@meridianindustrial.com>
Date:
Subject: PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL — Attorney-Client Communication: Engagement
 to Prepare Compromise ICA Terms — Intercreditor Agreement Negotiations
Content-Type: text/plain; charset="utf-8"
Content-Transfer-Encoding: quoted-printable
MIME-Version: 1.0

---

**PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL =E2=80=94 ATTORNEY-CLIENT COMMUNICATION**

**This email and any attachments are protected by the attorney-client privile=
ge and the work product doctrine. Do not forward or distribute without expres=
s authorization.**

---

David,

I am writing to formally engage you and KTM to prepare proposed compromise la=
nguage for the four contested provisions in our intercreditor agreement negot=
iations. Time is extremely short, and I need this turned around quickly. I am=
 copying Rachel Fong, our General Counsel, who will be your day-to-day contac=
t on process questions, but I want to lay out the full scope of the assignmen=
t and my thinking directly.

**I. Background and Current Impasse**

As you know, ICA negotiations among Meridian (as Borrower), Cartwright Nation=
al Bank (as First Lien Agent), and Ridgeline Capital Partners (as Second Lien=
 Agent) commenced on May 12, 2025. As of today, we have reached an impasse on=
 four key ICA provisions, and the negotiations have effectively stalled.

For context on the broader deal: Meridian is entering into a $475 million Fir=
st Lien Credit Facility =E2=80=94 consisting of a $125 million Revolver and a=
 $350 million Term Loan A =E2=80=94 with a syndicate of seven first lien lend=
ers led by Cartwright, and a $150 million Second Lien Term Loan B with three =
second lien lenders led by Ridgeline. Total secured debt is $625 million. The=
 1L Credit Facility matures June 15, 2030; the 2L Term Loan matures June 15, =
2031. Pricing is SOFR + 250 bps on the first lien and SOFR + 625 bps on the s=
econd lien. All documents are governed by New York law.

For your reference, Cartwright's outside counsel is Thomas Buchanan III at Al=
dgate & Pryor LLP, and Ridgeline's counsel is Naomi Oduya at Sternfeld Yates =
& Carroll LLP.

**II. Urgency =E2=80=94 Drop-Dead Date and Existing Facility Maturity**

I cannot overstate the urgency here. Our **drop-dead date for closing the ent=
ire financing is July 31, 2025** =E2=80=94 approximately **34 days from today=
**, and even fewer from the date your memorandum would be circulated to the p=
arties. More critically, **Meridian's existing credit facility matures on Aug=
ust 15, 2025**. If we fail to close by July 31, we will be dangerously close =
to maturity of the existing facility with no replacement financing in place. =
That is not a position I am willing to put this company in.

This timeline constraint must be front and center in everything KTM prepares.=
 The compromise terms need to be credible and closeable =E2=80=94 we do not h=
ave time for another round of positional back-and-forth.

**III. The Four Contested Provisions**

Below is my summary of the impasse on each of the four provisions, drawing on=
 the position letters from Cartwright/Aldgate and Ridgeline/Sternfeld (which =
I am attaching to this email) as well as our prior discussions.

**(i) Standstill Period =E2=80=94 Section 3.01(b)**

This is the widest gap and likely the most contentious issue. Cartwright prop=
oses a **270-day** standstill period. Ridgeline proposes **90 days**. Cartwri=
ght further insists that there should be **no early termination events** duri=
ng the standstill =E2=80=94 a flat 270-day clock with no outs.

From prior discussions, I understand that Cartwright's credit committee has a=
 minimum acceptable standstill of **150 days**, and Ridgeline has indicated i=
t could accept up to **180 days** but only with meaningful early termination =
triggers. Your own KTM market survey showed a **median standstill of 180 days=
** in comparable ICAs from 2022=E2=80=932024, which I think is useful framing=
. I would expect the landing zone to be somewhere in the 150=E2=80=93180 day =
range, but the early termination question is where the real fight will be. Pl=
ease give this careful thought.

**(ii) Second Lien Purchase Right =E2=80=94 Section 5.01**

This provision has two sub-issues:

*Purchase Price:* Cartwright insists on par =E2=80=94 100% of outstanding pri=
ncipal plus accrued interest at the **default rate**, plus all fees, plus **m=
andatory assumption of unfunded revolver commitments**. Ridgeline insists on =
the lesser of par or fair market value determined by an independent dealer po=
ll.

Cartwright has signaled that **par for funded debt** is "non-negotiable," and=
 I think we should accept that as a given. Ridgeline has indicated it could a=
ccept par for funded debt **if** assumption of the unfunded revolver commitme=
nts is **optional rather than mandatory**. Ridgeline has explained =E2=80=94 =
and I find this credible =E2=80=94 that its fund's **limited partnership agre=
ement imposes constraints** that prevent it from taking on open-ended revolvi=
ng credit commitment obligations. This is a structural issue for them, not ju=
st a negotiating position.

To put numbers on this: at the non-default contract rate (SOFR at 5.33% plus =
250 bps gives an all-in rate of approximately 7.83%), 90 days of accrued inte=
rest on the $475 million 1L facility would be approximately $9.30 million, ma=
king an illustrative par purchase price roughly $484.30 million for funded de=
bt alone. The 1L Revolver commitment is $125 million =E2=80=94 this is a very=
 significant contingent obligation if assumption is mandatory.

One area for compromise may be **accrued interest at the non-default contract=
 rate** rather than the default rate. Please evaluate this.

**(iii) Permitted Second Lien Refinancing Indebtedness =E2=80=94 Section 2.03=
(c)**

Three sub-issues here:

*Principal increase cap:* Cartwright insists on a **0% increase** =E2=80=94 t=
he refinancing must be for equal or lesser principal. Ridgeline wants up to a=
 **20% increase** ($30 million on the $150 million 2L facility). Ridgeline ha=
s signaled it could accept a **10% cap** ($15 million) if the increase is lim=
ited to covering refinancing fees and transaction costs. I think 10% with app=
ropriate limitations is a reasonable landing point, but I want your view.

*Maturity floor:* Cartwright wants the refinancing maturity to be no earlier =
than **September 14, 2030** =E2=80=94 91 days after the 1L maturity of June 1=
5, 2030. Ridgeline wants the floor to be the **original 2L maturity of June 1=
5, 2031**. I note that the original 2L maturity already exceeds the 91-day po=
st-1L date by a significant margin, so Ridgeline's position is effectively mo=
re restrictive in practice. I want you to analyze whether Ridgeline's positio=
n actually disadvantages Cartwright in any scenario, and if not, whether ther=
e is a way to frame this that satisfies both sides.

*Restrictiveness test:* Cartwright wants a blanket "no more restrictive in an=
y material respect" test applied to refinancing indebtedness. Ridgeline wants=
 no restrictiveness test at all. Cartwright has signaled flexibility here =E2=
=80=94 it would accept a **narrowed restrictiveness test** limited to specifi=
ed provisions if paired with a **most-favored-nation (MFN) right** allowing C=
artwright to benefit from any more favorable terms in the refinancing documen=
tation. For context, the existing 2L financial maintenance covenant is a **To=
tal Leverage ratio not to exceed 6.5x**. Please consider how a narrowed test =
plus MFN could work in practice.

**(iv) Application of Asset Disposition and Casualty Proceeds =E2=80=94 Secti=
on 4.02**

Cartwright insists on a **100% sweep to 1L** of all asset sale and casualty/i=
nsurance proceeds, overriding any reinvestment rights. Ridgeline wants the IC=
A to **follow the reinvestment mechanics** in the 1L Credit Agreement, with a=
ny residual proceeds (after 1L reinvestment or paydown) flowing to 2L.

The 1L Credit Agreement's own terms include: a $5 million individual threshol=
d and a $15 million annual aggregate threshold for asset sale prepayment trig=
gers; a 365-day reinvestment period; and a 100% sweep of non-reinvested proce=
eds.

My understanding is that Cartwright would accept **reinvestment rights for sm=
aller ordinary-course asset sales** but insists on a **hard sweep for large o=
r extraordinary dispositions and casualty events**. Ridgeline's minimum posit=
ion is that there must be **some form of 2L access to residual proceeds** =E2=
=80=94 proceeds that remain after the 1L obligations have been satisfied or a=
fter the reinvestment period has expired.

An ordinary-course threshold of **$20 million** has been discussed informally=
. If we use a threshold-based approach, I want you to ensure that any anti-ev=
asion protections are robust =E2=80=94 the compromise should address the risk=
 of structured sales designed to stay below the threshold through sequencing =
or related transactions.

**IV. Instructions**

Here is what I need from you:

1. **Prepare proposed compromise language** for each of the four contested IC=
A provisions identified above (Sections 3.01(b), 5.01, 2.03(c), and 4.02).

2. **Present the compromises as a single, integrated package.** This is delib=
erate and important. I do not want these offered piecemeal. Concessions on on=
e issue are linked to concessions on others, and unbundling the package could=
 undermine the overall balance we are trying to achieve. Please draft accordi=
ngly =E2=80=94 the memorandum should frame the four proposals as an interdepe=
ndent set.

3. **For each proposed term, provide negotiation rationale** explaining why t=
he compromise is reasonable and supportable =E2=80=94 what each side gains, w=
hat each side gives up, and why the position should be acceptable to both age=
nts and their respective lender groups.

4. **Deliver the memorandum to me by June 27, 2025** so I can review it befor=
e any further discussions with the agents or their counsel. The deliverable s=
hould be in the form of a written memorandum marked Privileged & Confidential.

**V. Meridian's Role in Breaking the Deadlock**

I want to be clear: **Meridian is willing to actively signal its support for =
the compromise package to both Cartwright and Ridgeline** to help break this =
deadlock. I want you to bear this in mind as you draft =E2=80=94 the compromi=
se should be something Meridian can genuinely endorse and advocate for with b=
oth agents. We are not trying to play the middle; we are trying to close a de=
al.

From the company's perspective, you should keep our financial position in min=
d. At approximately **$89.4 million LTM EBITDA**, we are looking at **5.31x f=
irst lien leverage** and **6.99x total leverage**, with a total loan-to-value=
 of roughly **97.66%**. The capital structure is tight, the equity cushion is=
 thin, and the company needs both lender groups fully committed and closing o=
n time. There is no room for one side to walk away. The tone of the compromis=
e needs to be balanced and fair =E2=80=94 we need to maintain strong working =
relationships with both Cartwright and Ridgeline going forward. These will be=
 our lenders for the next five to six years.

**VI. Attachments**

I am attaching the following for your reference:

1. Cartwright National Bank / Aldgate & Pryor LLP =E2=80=94 Position Letter o=
n Contested ICA Provisions
2. Ridgeline Capital Partners / Sternfeld Yates & Carroll LLP =E2=80=94 Posit=
ion Letter on Contested ICA Provisions

You and the KTM team already have the drafts of the 1L Credit Agreement, 2L C=
redit Agreement, and the working ICA draft on file. Please let me know if you=
 need updated versions of any of those documents.

**VII. Next Steps**

Please call me or Rachel if you have any questions before proceeding. Rachel =
can be reached at (704) 555-0188 or on her cell. I am available tomorrow morn=
ing before 10:00 a.m. ET and again after 3:00 p.m.

I want to reiterate: **every day matters.** We have 34 days until the drop-de=
ad date and even less time to circulate a credible compromise and negotiate t=
o closure. I need KTM moving on this immediately.

Thank you, David. I appreciate the effort and focus from you and your team on=
 this.

Best regards,

**Sandra Koh-Wexler**
Chief Executive Officer
Meridian Industrial Holdings, Inc.
4200 Tryon Industrial Parkway
Charlotte, NC 28217
